claims.text: Ed and Lorraine Warren are the most famous paranormal investigators in history and have worked on more than 10,000 cases.

evidence.quote:
Ed and Lorraine Warren are maybe the
most famous paranormal investigators in
history

claims.rationale: The Warrens are indeed among the most famous paranormal investigators thanks to the Conjuring franchise. However the host later explicitly debunks the 10,000-cases claim: 'if they did one case a day without a day off that would take them 27 years'. Medium directTruth reflects partial truth (fame yes, case count no).

claims.text: Ronald 'Butch' DeFeo Jr. claimed he heard voices urging him to kill his family in the 1974 Amityville murders.

evidence.quote:
Butch
claimed he heard voices urging him to
kill

claims.rationale: Ronald DeFeo Jr. was convicted of the November 13, 1974 killings at 112 Ocean Avenue; his 'voices' insanity-defense claim is a matter of court record. Whether he actually heard voices is untestable; that he claimed them is fact.

claims.text: The Amityville haunting story was fabricated during a meeting between defense lawyer William Weber and the Lutz family.

evidence.quote:
the Amityville story was actually
created by Butch defeo's lawyer William
Weber

The Amityville
Horror Story is definitely debunked

claims.rationale: William Weber publicly admitted in 1979 that the Amityville Horror story was largely invented with the Lutzes over several bottles of wine. This is corroborated by a federal civil suit (1979) in which Weber sued the Lutzes. Strong documentary basis.

dependencies.rationale: The fabricated haunting story was built on the real DeFeo murders as a prop for the insanity defense.

claims.text: The Lutzes' claim of seeing hoof prints in the snow is contradicted by weather records showing no snow at that time.

evidence.quote:
the lutzes
couldn't have seen hoof prints in the
snow because there was no snow

claims.rationale: Weather-record check on Amityville NY for December 1975 / January 1976 is a standard skeptical rebuttal and confirms no relevant snowfall. Clean factual check.

dependencies.rationale: Independent factual contradiction of a specific Lutz claim reinforces the broader hoax finding.

claims.text: Ed Warren was the only layperson recognized by the Catholic Church as a demonologist.

evidence.quote:
Ed
Warren was the only lay person
recognized by the church as a
demonologist

claims.rationale: This is a widely repeated Warren self-description; there is no formal Catholic Church credential of 'demonologist' for laypeople, and no diocesan record supports Ed's claim. Host later notes Ed was 'a self-proclaimed demonologist' — indicating that the church recognition is dubious.

claims.text: Ed Warren was a self-proclaimed demonologist rather than someone formally recognized by the Catholic Church.

evidence.quote:
Ed
Warren was a self-proclaimed
demonologist

claims.rationale: The host's own closing verdict. 'Self-proclaimed' is the standard neutral description used by skeptical sources; no independent Catholic credentialing body has ever confirmed Ed's 'demonologist' status.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Direct conflict — the earlier Warren framing vs the host's verdict that Ed's demonologist status was self-proclaimed.

claims.text: The Annabelle doll story is a beat-for-beat retelling of the 1963 Twilight Zone episode 'Living Doll'.

evidence.quote:
it's basically a retelling beat
for Beat of The Twilight Zone episode
living doll which aired in 1963 on CBS

claims.rationale: 'Living Doll' (Twilight Zone S5E6, November 1963) does have structural similarities to the Annabelle story — a malevolent doll threatening a family. The mother's name in the episode was 'Annabelle', a striking coincidence. The 'beat-for-beat' claim is a stronger statement than the actual similarity supports; structural parallels exist but the two are not identical.

claims.text: Bathsheba Sherman never lived in the Harrisville farmhouse, never killed anyone, and by all accounts was a good person.

evidence.quote:
Bathsheba was a real person but that's
about all the story gets right she never
lived in that house never killed anyone

claims.rationale: Bathsheba Sherman's genealogy has been researched by skeptic writers and current homeowner Norma Sutcliffe; Sherman died in 1885 at age 73 and was never charged with or convicted of any crime. The 'witch' attribution is pure Conjuring-franchise fabrication.

claims.text: The Enfield Poltergeist began in August 1977 when Peggy Hodgson's children reported furniture moving on its own.

evidence.quote:
August 1977 Enfield North London this is
considered one of the events that proves
the existence of poltergeists Peggy
Hodgson had just put her children to bed
when she heard the sound of a chair
being dragged

claims.rationale: The Enfield case is extensively documented by SPR investigator Maurice Grosse and writer Guy Lyon Playfair; the initiating August 1977 chair/dresser reports, and police involvement, are in the official record.

claims.text: Some of the Enfield phenomena were hoaxed — Janet admitted faking and the children were caught faking paranormal incidents.

evidence.quote:
Janet admitted to it and the
kids were caught multiple times faking
so-called paranormal incidents

claims.rationale: Janet Hodgson publicly admitted in later interviews that they faked some phenomena (estimated 2% by her count). Investigators also caught the children faking on multiple occasions. Documented in the SPR files and Will Storr's This Much Is True.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] Known faking weakens but does not eliminate the paranormal interpretation — the question becomes which (if any) incidents were genuine.

claims.text: Ed Warren told ghostwriter Ray Garton to fabricate material for the Snedeker book — 'use what works and just make the rest up'.

evidence.quote:
use what works
and just make the rest up just make it
scary and this was not the first time Ed
Warren hired a horror writer for help
not a journalist not a biographer

claims.rationale: Ray Garton has been on the record for decades stating that Ed Warren told him to make up scary content for In a Dark Place. His public statements predate the Conjuring franchise and he has no financial incentive to lie.

claims.text: The Snedekers lived in the former funeral home for two years without incident, and the haunting story began only after they fell behind on rent.

evidence.quote:
the snedeker's lived in the house
for two years without an incident

claims.rationale: The timing coincidence with eviction proceedings is documented in contemporary Connecticut press coverage (e.g., the Carmen Snedeker lawsuit and landlord statements). Strong circumstantial evidence that the haunting was a cover story for eviction defense.

dependencies.rationale: Both facts point to the Snedeker haunting being fabricated rather than experienced.

claims.text: Ed Warren initiated a sexual relationship with Judith Penny when she was 15 years old and the affair continued for over 40 years while Lorraine knew.

evidence.quote:
Judith Penny Ed Warren
initiated a relationship with her when
she was only 15

claims.rationale: Judith Penny's sworn testimony is the direct source. Warner Brothers' non-disparagement contract language (specifically prohibiting depiction of minor relationships) is strong circumstantial corroboration. The Warrens' daughter disputes the account; absence of prosecution keeps directTruth below 0.9.

claims.text: No case the Warrens investigated was ever supported by evidence beyond their word.

evidence.quote:
no case that the Warrens
worked on was ever supported by any
evidence besides their word

claims.rationale: Host's closing evaluation matches the skeptical literature consensus (Joe Nickell, Benjamin Radford, etc.) — no Warren case has produced physical evidence that survived independent scrutiny. Strong directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] If none of the cases is independently supported, the Warrens' fame is earned on story-telling rather than investigation.

claims.text: The famous 'Lady in White' ghost video was staged — Ed Warren had Judith Penny put on a white dress and walk around a cemetery.

evidence.quote:
put on
a white dress and walk around a cemetery

claims.rationale: Judith Penny's testimony identifies herself as the figure in the Lady in White video. No independent analysis of the video contradicts the identification. Directly undermines one of Ed's most-circulated pieces of 'evidence'.

dependencies.rationale: Concrete case of Warren-produced 'evidence' shown to be staged.
